title: Timeout with time.After
instruction:
Never wait forever. `time.After(d)` returns a channel that receives one value after duration `d`.

Use it in a `select` as a deadline:

```
select {
case result := <-work:
    fmt.Println(result)
case <-time.After(2 * time.Second):
    fmt.Println("timed out")
}
```

Run this. The slow operation takes 200ms but the timeout is only 50ms — so the timeout fires. Then change the timeout to `300ms` so the real result arrives instead.
hint: Change 50 * time.Millisecond to 300 * time.Millisecond in the time.After call.

codeChecks.message: Change the timeout to 300 * time.Millisecond so the result arrives in time.
Replace the TODO comment with your change.

successMessage: 300ms timeout, 200ms operation — result arrived in time. Timeouts are non-negotiable in production. A service without timeouts will eventually hang forever waiting for a dead server.

title: time.Ticker — do something repeatedly
instruction:
`time.Ticker` fires on a regular interval. Unlike `time.After` which fires once, a ticker fires forever until you stop it.

```
ticker := time.NewTicker(500 * time.Millisecond)
defer ticker.Stop()
for t := range ticker.C {
    fmt.Println("tick at", t)
}
```

Run this. A ticker fires every 50ms. A done channel stops it after 3 ticks. Then add a line that prints `"Ticker stopped"` after the loop.
hint: After the for loop (outside it), add: fmt.Println("Ticker stopped")

codeChecks.message: Print "Ticker stopped" after the loop exits.
Replace the TODO comment with your print statement.

successMessage: Tickers power heartbeats, polling loops, scheduled jobs, and health checks. Always call ticker.Stop() when you are done — an unstopped ticker leaks a goroutine.

title: Fan-in — merge multiple channels
instruction:
Fan-in merges multiple channels into one. Any value from any input channel flows out the single output channel.

Useful when you have multiple data sources (sensors, APIs, workers) and want to process everything in one place.

Run this. Two sources send numbers. `merge` combines them. Add a third source `source(7, 8, 9)` to the merge call.
hint: merged := merge(ch1, ch2, source(7, 8, 9))  — add source(7,8,9) as a third argument to merge.

codeChecks.message: Add source(7, 8, 9) as a third input to merge.
Replace the TODO comment with your updated merge call.

successMessage: 1+2+...+9 = 45. Three sources, one stream, one total. Fan-in is how you aggregate results from multiple concurrent workers or external services.

title: Fan-out — distribute work
instruction:
Fan-out distributes jobs from one channel to multiple worker goroutines.

You used this in the worker pool chapter. Here is the clean abstraction:

- One `jobs` channel sends work
- N workers each read from `jobs`
- First free worker takes the next job

Run this. 3 workers process 9 jobs. Add 3 more workers (total 6) and see if the jobs finish faster.
hint: Change for w := 1; w <= 3; w++ to w <= 6 to add 3 more workers.

codeChecks.message: Change the worker count to 6: w <= 6
Replace the TODO comment with your change.

successMessage: 9 jobs, 6 workers — more workers means jobs complete faster when they are I/O-bound. Fan-out is the foundation of every concurrent task processor, HTTP server, and job queue in Go.

title: Pipeline with cancellation
instruction:
A pipeline is a series of stages connected by channels. When you want to cancel the whole pipeline early, pass a `done` channel to every stage.

When `done` is closed, every stage sees it via `select` and exits.

Run this. The pipeline generates → squares → prints. Close `done` after 3 values to stop early.
successMessage: The pipeline produced 3 values then stopped cleanly — no goroutine leaks. When done closes, every stage sees it and exits. This is the correct way to cancel a multi-stage pipeline.

title: context.WithTimeout
instruction:
`context` is the standard Go way to carry deadlines and cancellation signals across function calls and goroutines.

```
ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 2*time.Second)
defer cancel()
```

Pass `ctx` to anything that might take too long. It automatically cancels when the deadline passes.

Run this. The slow operation checks `ctx.Done()`. Change the timeout from `50ms` to `300ms` so the operation completes.
hint: Change 50 * time.Millisecond to 300 * time.Millisecond in context.WithTimeout.

codeChecks.message: Change the timeout to 300 * time.Millisecond.
Replace the TODO comment with your change.

successMessage: context.WithTimeout is the standard way to add deadlines to database calls, HTTP requests, and any slow operation. Always defer cancel() — it frees resources even if the context expires naturally.

title: context.WithCancel
instruction:
`context.WithCancel` gives you manual control. Call `cancel()` whenever you decide to stop.

This is how HTTP servers cancel requests when the client disconnects, how search engines stop slower queries when a faster one returns, and how services shut down gracefully.

Run this. Two workers race to complete. The first to finish cancels the context — stopping the other.
successMessage: Worker 1 won the race. cancel() signalled Worker 2 to stop immediately. This "first result wins, cancel the rest" pattern is called early exit and is used in distributed systems everywhere.

title: Rate limiting
instruction:
Rate limiting controls how fast work is processed — to avoid overwhelming a database, an API, or a downstream service.

A time.Ticker acts as a token dispenser. Each tick is a token that allows one operation.

Run this. 5 requests are processed but only 1 per 50ms is allowed — the ticker controls the pace. Add two more requests to `requests` (strings 6 and 7).

codeChecks.message: Add "request 6" to the requests channel.
Add "request 7" to the requests channel.
Replace the TODO comment with your two requests.

successMessage: One request per tick, smooth and controlled. Rate limiting protects your services from being overwhelmed. The ticker pattern is simple and effective — no third-party library needed.

title: Semaphore — limit concurrent access
instruction:
A semaphore limits how many goroutines can do something at the same time.

Implement it with a buffered channel: send to acquire, receive to release. The buffer size is the limit.

```
sem := make(chan struct{}, 3)  // max 3 concurrent
sem <- struct{}{}              // acquire
defer func() { <-sem }()      // release
```

Run this. 10 goroutines try to run but only 3 can run at once. The semaphore enforces the limit.
expectedOutput: All goroutines done, max 3 ran concurrently
successMessage: 10 goroutines, max 3 at once, no violations. Semaphores limit access to scarce resources: database connection pools, file handles, API rate limits. A buffered channel is all you need.

title: Graceful shutdown
instruction:
When your server receives a shutdown signal (Ctrl+C, SIGTERM from Kubernetes), you need to:
1. Stop accepting new work
2. Finish any in-progress work
3. Exit cleanly

Run this. The server processes jobs. When `shutdown` is closed, it finishes the current job then exits — no work is abandoned.
successMessage: No jobs lost. The server received the shutdown signal, finished what it was doing, drained the queue, then exited. Graceful shutdown is essential in production — abrupt exits lose data and break clients.

title: Fix a goroutine leak
instruction:
A goroutine leak is a goroutine that starts but never exits. Over time it consumes memory and eventually crashes your program.

The most common cause: a goroutine blocks on a channel that is never closed and never has a receiver.

The code below leaks a goroutine — `work` sends a result but nobody closes `done`, so the goroutine waits forever.

Fix it: close the `done` channel after reading the result.
hint: Add close(done) after result := <-ch  (right before the Println).

codeChecks.message: Close the done channel: close(done)
Replace the TODO comment with close(done).

successMessage: Worker exited cleanly. One close(done) and the leak is fixed. Always ask: for every goroutine I start, what will cause it to exit? If you cannot answer that, you have a potential leak.

title: Build a real-time event processor
instruction:
No starter code.

Build an event processor:

1. Define `Event` struct with `Source` string and `Value` int
2. Write two `emitter` functions — each sends 3 events on its own channel then closes it
3. Use fan-in (`merge`) to combine both emitters into one channel
4. Process events with a 500ms overall timeout using `context.WithTimeout`
5. Print each event as `"[Source] Value: X"` — stop if the context times out
6. Print `"Processed N events"` at the end

Expected output processes all 6 events (or fewer if timeout fires).
hint: Use context.WithTimeout, merge both emitter channels, select on merged channel and ctx.Done() in the loop.

codeChecks.message: Define an Event struct.
Write emitter functions that return <-chan Event.
Use context.WithTimeout for the overall deadline.
Use a WaitGroup or merge function to combine channels.
Check ctx.Done() to handle timeout.
Print the final count of processed events.

successMessage: Fan-in, timeout, context cancellation — all working together. This is exactly what a real-time event ingestion service looks like in production. Kafka consumers, WebSocket handlers, sensor data pipelines — same pattern. You are done.
